title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered as adult literacy tutors. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Opening the Pages: Adult Literacy Tutors

texts.A:
### Julian, The Library Program
'I thought I’d just be teaching the alphabet, but it was much deeper,' Julian says. [51]The hardest part was the initial barrier of shame that many students felt, which we had to overcome before any learning could happen.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my own verbal communication skills in my job as a salesperson became much more empathetic and clear.[/48] [53]I learned that literacy isn't just a skill; it's the foundation of dignity and civic participation.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in marketing helped me find creative ways to make the lessons more engaging and relevant to their lives.[/50] [47]Initially, the emotional intensity of the students' stories was quite a shock to my system.[/47] It was a humbling chapter.
texts.B:
### Sarah, The Workplace Initiative
Sarah helped colleagues improve their reading skills. [49]The experience taught me that we often overlook the invisible struggles of those who have spent a lifetime hiding their difficulties.[/49] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep sense of joy I’d feel when a student read their first book to their own children.[/52] [47]I was quite startled by how much the mental effort of tailoring lessons to different learning styles exhausted me in the first month.[/47] [54]What I valued most was the courage of the students, who were willing to be vulnerable in front of their peers.[/54] [56]If I hadn't seen a flyer in the staff room, I never would have realized there was a need for this.[/56] It was a human connection.
texts.C:
### Victor, The Prison Education Project
Victor taught reading and writing in a correctional facility. [51]I struggled with the environment; the constant noise and security protocols made it difficult to create a quiet space for learning.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general patience and ability to handle high-pressure situations reached an all-time high, which was a great side effect.[/48] [53]I realized that stories are a way for people to transcend their physical surroundings, regardless of their past.[/53] [50]I found that the discipline I used in my previous career as a police officer was perfect for navigating the complex rules of the facility.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the stark, windowless classrooms, which I found quite oppressive at first.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried I wouldn't be able to relate to the students.
texts.D:
### Elena, The Digital Literacy Swap
'I taught senior citizens how to navigate the internet and email,' Elena says. [49]The project made me realize that the 'digital divide' is a form of social isolation that we need to bridge urgently.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have found the program if my local council hadn't invited me to join their community outreach team.[/56] [54]I was moved by the persistence of the seniors, who were undeterred by their initial fear of the technology.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the physical strain of leaning over screens for hours to be so draining initially.[/47] [55]I found the initial task of explaining abstract concepts like 'the cloud' incredibly frustrating and difficult.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I would be too impatient, but their enthusiasm was infectious.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or psychological impact of the tutoring environment?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) mentions the emotional intensity, Sarah (B) the mental effort of tailoring lessons, and Elena (D) the physical strain.

questions.question: notes a positive change in their professional communication or personal resilience?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) saw his salesperson communication improve, and Victor (C) found his patience reached an all-time high.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perspective on invisible struggles or social isolation?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) reflects on invisible struggles, and Elena (D) on the digital divide as social isolation.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or career skills were advantageous to the tutoring?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) used his marketing background, and Victor (C) his police officer discipline.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to the students' emotions or the physical learning environment?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) mentions the barrier of shame, and Victor (C) the noise and security protocols.

questions.question: admits to having had prior doubts or having a strong emotional reaction to a student's success?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) felt joy when a student read to their kids, and Victor (C) worried about relating to students.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about dignity or the power of stories?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) saw literacy as the foundation of dignity, and Victor (C) saw stories as a way to transcend surroundings.

questions.question: highlights the value of the courage or persistence of the students themselves?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) valued the courage of students, and Elena (D) the persistence of the seniors.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the physical setting or abstract concepts?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) adjusted to windowless classrooms, and Elena (D) found explaining abstract concepts difficult.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external trigger or invitation that led to their involvement?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) saw a flyer in the staff room, and Elena (D) was invited by the local council.
